EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the region with steady southerly flow pushing water up into coastal communities both along the ocean and within the Delaware River/Bay and Chesapeake Bay. Water levels on the tidal Delaware River were higher due to 2 to 4+ inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding was reported in the tidal areas of the county, and there were numerous road closures. Many homes and other buildings were surrounded by flood waters with some minor property damage occurring. The tide gauge at Dewey Beach reached 4.44 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Fenwick Island reached 3.54 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at South Bethany Beach reached 3.89 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary just south of the region resulted in stagnant, moist air across the area. Warm air lifted over the boundary facilitated the development of dense fog late on the 26th. The fog dissipated early on the 27th as the front shifted south and drier air advected into the area from the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog with visibility 1/4 mile or below was observed for several hours at Delaware Coastal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure moving northeastward across the Great Lakes brought strong southeasterly winds to the region. Winds peaked during the evening of January 9th. Widespread reports of scattered tree damage were received from across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delaware Coastal Airport reported a peak gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked to the northwest of the forecast area on Tuesday into early Wednesday (January 9-10th). A cold front associated with the low swept through the region on Wednesday. In the system���s wake, total rainfall amounts varied from about 1.50 inches up to about 4.00 inches. The rain fell on already saturated soils and there was some snow on the ground that melted and also contributed to the runoff.  As a result, river flooding was prevalent across the Passaic Basin, Raritan Basin, and southeast Pennsylvania basins including the Schuylkill and Brandywine. The Brandywine flooding extended in to Delaware. Smaller streams and rivers across northern New Castle County in Delaware, such as the Christina, Red Clay, and White Clay also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across southeast Pennsylvania and northern Delaware, flooding occurred on the Brandywine River, the Schuylkill River, the Christina River, Red Clay Creek, and White Clay Creek.  The Cooch���s Bridge gauge crested at 12.52 ft. The Langhorne gauge crested at 13.91 ft. The Downingtown gauge crested at 11.41 ft. The Newark gauge crested at 15.14 ft. The Chadd���s Ford gauge crested at 12.84 ft. The Wooddale gauge crested at 9.03 ft. The Graterford gauge crested at 16.30 ft. The Chester gauge crested at 12.55 ft. The Philadelphia gauge crested at 12.13 ft. The Pottstown gauge crested at 13.65 ft. The Norristown gauge crested at 16.29 ft. The Wilmington gauge crested at 17.39 ft. All of these crests are above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the region with steady southerly flow pushing water up into coastal communities both along the ocean and within the Delaware River/Bay and Chesapeake Bay. Water levels on the tidal Delaware River were higher due to 2 to 4+ inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to major flooding occurred in the tidal areas of New Castle County. There were many road closures with the flood waters affecting numerous homes and businesses. The tide gauge at Newport reached a level of 9.64 feet MLLW. The tide gauge at Wilmington reached a level of 9.31 feet MLLW. The tide gauge at Delaware City reached a level of 9.06 feet MLLW. The tide gauge at Reedy Point reached a level of 9.12 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southeast winds resulted in widespread tidal flooding for much of the coast line and tidal waterways. The strong southeast to south winds resulted in a push of water up the Delaware Bay/River and through the back bays of the Atlantic coast. The outcome was the tidal inundation of roads and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some roadway flooding was reported in the tidal areas of the county, and there were some road closures. Some homes and other buildings were surrounded by flood waters with some minor property damage occurring. The tide gauge at Indian River Inlet reached 5.21 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread light snowfall accumulations across the region, with a narrow mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurring across eastern Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. A coating of snow also occurred well north and south of the band with amounts around 1 to 3 across the upper half of the Delmarva peninsula, even a dusting less than 1 down into southern Delaware. The snow generally fell within about 3-4 hours, however impacts were limited as roadway temperatures hovered near freezing and the snowfall occurred late on a Friday night and early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged largely from around 1 to 3 across the county. The highest snowfall report was from Delaware City with 3.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developing along a stationary front resulted in a few showers and thunderstorms developing. A few storms were severe producing damaging wind gusts and a tornado. Additionally training storms led to flash flooding in northern Delaware.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was noted starting in Weer Circle. Along Old Capitol Trail, a small tree was snapped at the trunk, and additional tree damage was noted near a commercial building between Old Capitol Trail and Kirkwood Highway. When it was in the area of Kirkwood Highway, there were multiple witnesses of the tornado. A business at a strip mall along Kirkwood Highway lost a portion of the roof. The tornado continued along a path towards the intersection of Farrand Drive and Hammond Place where there was damage to a free standing light pole. Additional damage, primarily uprooted trees was noted in the Sherwood Park development. The last observed damage was noted near West Robino Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation falling into a cold airmass resulted in some light wintry precipitation accumulations of a light glaze of freezing rain, sleet, and snow from around portions of northern Delmarva, the Philly metro, and northeast into northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania. Much of the light icing remained within the New Jersey portions of the Philly metro and northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of sleet, flurries, and light freezing rain lasted for about 1-2 hours. A dusting of snow and sleet accumulation and and a light glaze of ice occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation falling into a cold airmass resulted in some light wintry precipitation accumulations of a light glaze of freezing rain, sleet, and snow from around portions of northern Delmarva, the Philly metro, and northeast into northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania. Much of the light icing remained within the New Jersey portions of the Philly metro and northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of sleet, flurries, and light freezing rain lasted for about 1-2 hours. A dusting of snow and sleet accumulation and and a light glaze of ice occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides associated with the full moon and a light onshore flow brought some moderate tidal flooding to the Delaware coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding was reported in the tidal areas of the county, and there were numerous road closures. Many homes and other buildings were surrounded by flood waters with some minor property damage occurring. The tide gauge at Lewes reached 7.02 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Indian River Inlet reached 5.33 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at Dewey Beach reached 4.30 feet MLLW.
